overview.body: You have built a lot this module. You have files that save and load game state, lists that track everywhere you have been, and a working save/reload cycle. This final week pulls it all together. You will replace the hardcoded hazard if/elif chain with a HAZARD_NAMES list, sort the hazard history alphabetically for display, calculate unique cities using the in operator, and write a formatted trip report to trip_report.txt at the end of a run. You will also add the final Module 3 function to function_library.py: display_numbered_list(). Before you begin: Copy your lab-3-2.py and function_library.py files into your working folder for this week. All changes go into new files named lab-3-3.py and an updated function_library.py. Do not overwrite your 3-2 work.

mentorQuote.quote: MASTER week is when it clicks. You stop adding features and start seeing the system. The trip report is not just output — it is proof that every piece you built this module is talking to every other piece.

objectives: Replace a hardcoded hazard if/elif chain with a HAZARD_NAMES list and index-based random selection
Use .copy() to duplicate a list before sorting so the original is preserved
Use .sort() to sort a list alphabetically in place
Build a unique cities list using the in operator to filter duplicates
Write a formatted multi-section report to a file using save_trip_report()
Add display_numbered_list() to function_library.py as the final Module 3 library function
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 7: Program 7-6 (list methods — remove), Program 7-9 (len and built-in functions), Program 7-12 (comprehensive list processing), Program 7-16 (writing a list to a file).
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 7
programRequirements: HAZARD_NAMES list constant defined with at least six hazard strings
get_random_hazard() uses random.randint() to pick an index and returns HAZARD_NAMES[index]
save_trip_report() writes the formatted report to trip_report.txt
Sorted hazards in the trip report use .copy() then .sort() on hazards_list
Unique cities are calculated using a for loop and the in operator
function_library.py adds display_numbered_list(items, title) with a docstring

namedConstants.name: HAZARD_NAMES
namedConstants.description: List of hazard name strings used for random selection

namedConstants.name: REPORT_FILE
namedConstants.description: String constant — filename for the trip report, always 'trip_report.txt'

overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-3-3.py and function_library.py this week.
overview.additionalFilesNote: function_library.py is submitted alongside lab-3-3.py. CodeGrade tests display_numbered_list() in isolation.

rows.task: Copy lab-3-2.py and function_library.py to lab-3-3.py
Replace hazard if/elif with list + get_random_hazard() function
Sort hazard display and build unique cities list
Write save_trip_report() and add REPORT_FILE constant
Add display_numbered_list() to library and wire up the full report
Test end-to-end, verify trip_report.txt, submit both files

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you are adding this week. The [REPLACED] tags show what changes from your 3.2 code.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
SAVE_FILE    = 'savegame.txt'             (unchanged)
REPORT_FILE  = 'trip_report.txt'          [NEW]
HAZARD_NAMES = [...]                      [NEW — replaces hazard if/elif]
SA_DESTINATIONS / SA_DISTANCES / SA_NOTES (unchanged)

# ---- HELPER FUNCTIONS ----
get_random_hazard()                       [REPLACED — uses HAZARD_NAMES[index]]
display_destinations()                    (unchanged)

# ---- REPORT FUNCTION ----
save_trip_report(player_name,
    current_city, total_miles, total_turns,
    hazards_faced, hazards_escaped,
    visited_cities, hazards_list)          [NEW]

# ---- SAVE / LOAD ----
save_game() / load_game()                 (unchanged from 3.2)

GAME LOOP:  (unchanged)
│
FINAL TRIP REPORT:
│── Unique cities calculation             [NEW]
│   └── for city in visited_cities:
│       └── if city not in unique_cities
│── sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy()  [NEW]
│── sorted_hazards.sort()                 [NEW]
│── function_library.display_numbered_list()  [NEW]
│── save_trip_report()                    [NEW]
│── Offer to save trip report to file     [NEW]

# ---- function_library.py additions ----
display_numbered_list(items, title)       [NEW]

checkpoints.title: Replace Hazard if/elif with HAZARD_NAMES List

content.body: Your hazard selection currently uses a chain of if/elif branches or a series of random number comparisons. You are going to apply the same parallel-list pattern from CP2 last week: define HAZARD_NAMES as a list constant, then write a get_random_hazard() function that picks a random index and returns the name at that position.

The if/elif chain for hazards is brittle — adding a new hazard means editing multiple places in your code. A list constant plus index-based selection means adding a hazard is one more string in the list. That is it. This is the same principle that cleaned up destination selection in Lab 3.2, and it will keep paying off every time the game grows.

content.quote: If you catch yourself writing elif again for something that's just picking from a list of options — stop. Make a list. Write one function. Every time I see a six-branch elif chain that could be a list, a junior dev somewhere is doubling their maintenance work.

items: Start by copying lab-3-2.py to a new file named lab-3-3.py. Also make a copy of your current function_library.py — both files get updated this week.
Near the top of lab-3-3.py, add the HAZARD_NAMES list constant with at least six hazard strings. Include the hazards already used in your game, plus any new ones you want to add.
Add the REPORT_FILE = 'trip_report.txt' constant nearby.
Define a get_random_hazard() function. It should use random.randint() to generate a random index in the range 0 to len(HAZARD_NAMES) - 1, then return HAZARD_NAMES[index].

In the game loop, find where hazards are triggered. Replace the old if/elif hazard selection logic with a call to get_random_hazard(). The return value is the hazard name string — use it the same way you used the old hazard variable.
Run the game and trigger a few hazards to confirm different hazard names appear and they all come from your list.

rows: Use len(HAZARD_NAMES) - 1 as the upper bound in random.randint(). That way adding a new hazard to the list automatically makes it reachable — you never have to update two places.
Don't hardcode the upper bound as a fixed number like 5. If your list grows to seven items but your randint still goes up to 5, the last item can never be selected.
Give get_random_hazard() a docstring — CodeGrade checks documentation on library-style functions.
Don't forget to remove or comment out the old if/elif hazard chain. Having both the old logic and the new function call active at the same time will cause hazards to behave unpredictably.

references.description: You created a list with initial values using sales = [0] * NUM_DAYS. HAZARD_NAMES uses the same idea — a list defined with its values at the top of the file.
You used random.randint(MIN, MAX) to simulate dice. get_random_hazard() uses the same call — just with 0 and len(HAZARD_NAMES) - 1 as the bounds.

content.body: Trigger at least three hazards during a test run. Confirm each one comes from your HAZARD_NAMES list. Then temporarily add a seventh hazard string to the list and verify it can appear — then remove it before submitting.

content.output:
A hazard appears on the road!
Speed trap!
Roll your d20 to escape! (You rolled: 14)
You escaped the hazard!

items: HAZARD_NAMES list constant defined with at least six strings
REPORT_FILE = 'trip_report.txt' constant defined
get_random_hazard() function defined with a docstring, using random index into HAZARD_NAMES
Old hazard if/elif chain removed from the game loop
Hazards still append to hazards_list as before

checkpoints.title: Add Sorted Hazard Display and Unique Cities Calculation

content.body: This checkpoint adds two list operations to the final trip report section. First, you will sort the hazard history alphabetically so it reads cleanly — but you must copy the list before sorting so the original encounter order is preserved. Second, you will calculate how many unique cities the player visited by filtering out duplicates using the in operator.

Sorted output makes reports readable — players can scan alphabetically for the hazards they remember. And the unique city count tells a more honest story than the total route length when a player visits the same city multiple times. These are also two of the most useful list patterns in Python: sort a copy to protect the original, and build a unique list with in to filter duplicates.

items: In the final trip report section of your game, add the sorted hazard display. First, make a copy of the list: sorted_hazards = hazards_list.copy(). Then sort the copy in place: sorted_hazards.sort(). Loop through sorted_hazards to display them — not hazards_list.
Add the unique cities calculation. Create an empty list unique_cities = []. Loop through visited_cities. For each city, check if city not in unique_cities — if it is not already there, append it.
Use len(unique_cities) in the trip report to display the count of unique cities visited.
Run the game, visit a city more than once, and confirm the unique count is lower than the total route length.

rows: hazards_list.copy() creates a new independent list. Sort the copy — the original stays in encounter order for saving.
sort() modifies the list permanently in place. Calling hazards_list.sort() directly means the save file will store hazards alphabetically instead of in the order they happened.
The unique cities pattern — if city not in unique_cities: unique_cities.append(city) — works on any list. It is the Chapter 7 way to deduplicate before set() arrives in Module 4.
Don't use sorted() (the built-in function) — it is not in scope this module. Use .copy() then .sort() on the copy.

references.description: You used the in operator to check if a product number was in a list. The unique cities check uses the exact same pattern: if city not in unique_cities.
Shows comprehensive list processing including sorting. The sort-a-copy discipline comes from the same instinct as protecting the original score list.

content.body: Visit at least one city more than once during your test run, and trigger at least two different hazards. Check that the on-screen trip report shows hazards in alphabetical order and that the unique city count is correct.

content.output:
HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (A-Z):
  1. Armadillo crossing!
  2. Buc-ee's appears!
  3. Speed trap!

Route: San Antonio -> Houston -> Austin -> Houston
Unique cities visited: 3

content.quote: Sorted hazards in a report are a small thing that makes a huge difference. Players notice when their journey feels organized. Copy first, sort the copy — that one line of discipline protects everything built underneath it.

items: Sorted hazard display uses hazards_list.copy() then .sort() — original list untouched
Unique cities are calculated with a for loop and in operator
len(unique_cities) appears in the trip report output

content.body: The trip report currently only prints to the screen. This checkpoint adds a save_trip_report() function that writes a formatted version to trip_report.txt so the player can keep a record of their run.

The on-screen report disappears when the session ends. Writing to trip_report.txt means the player can share their run, compare routes, or just feel the weight of what they accomplished. It is also the first time you are writing a multi-section structured document to a file — a pattern that scales directly to real data export in professional software.

content.quote: A deliverable that only exists on screen isn't really a deliverable. The client asked for something they can hold onto. trip_report.txt is your handoff artifact — treat the format like it matters, because to them it does.

items: Define save_trip_report() with the following parameters: player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns, hazards_faced, hazards_escaped, visited_cities, and hazards_list. Give it a docstring.
Inside the function, open REPORT_FILE in write mode using the with statement.
Write the header section: a title line, a separator line of equal signs, the driver name, and the final destination.
Write a ROUTE section. Loop through visited_cities and write each city separated by ' -> ' on a single line. Then write the unique cities count on the next line.
Write a STATISTICS section with total miles, total turns, hazards faced, hazards escaped, and the escape rate as a percentage (use :.1f formatting). Guard against division by zero on the escape rate.
Write a HAZARDS (A-Z) section. Copy and sort hazards_list inside the function, then write each hazard prefixed with ' - '.
Write a closing separator line.

In the main game, after the trip report prints to screen, ask the player if they want to save the report using function_library.get_yes_no(). If yes, call save_trip_report() with all the required arguments.

rows: Use with open(REPORT_FILE, 'w') inside the function — not the string literal 'trip_report.txt'. That is what the constant is for.
Division by zero on the escape rate. Guard with if hazards_faced > 0 before calculating the percentage, and default to 0.0 when no hazards were triggered.
Build the route line by joining visited_cities with ' -> ' using a loop or the join() string method — either works at this scope level.
Opening in 'w' mode overwrites the file every time. That is intentional — the player saves a fresh report at the end of each run. Do not use 'a' (append) mode.

content.output:
DEEP IN THE HEART - TRIP REPORT
========================================
Driver: Maria
Final Destination: Galveston

ROUTE:
  San Antonio -> Houston -> Galveston
  Unique cities: 3

STATISTICS:
  Total Miles: 267
  Total Turns: 4
  Hazards Faced: 2
  Hazards Escaped: 1
  Escape Rate: 50.0%

HAZARDS (A-Z):
 - Armadillo crossing!
 - Buc-ee's appears!

========================================

references.description: Shows the pattern for writing list contents to a file with a loop — adapt this for writing the hazards section.

content.body: End a complete game and choose to save the report. Open trip_report.txt in a text editor and confirm all four sections appear with correct formatting. Run the game again and save another report — confirm the old one is overwritten cleanly (write mode, not append).

items: save_trip_report() defined with all parameters and a docstring
Function uses with open(REPORT_FILE, 'w') — not the constant string directly
Report contains header, ROUTE, STATISTICS, and HAZARDS (A-Z) sections in that order
Escape rate is formatted to one decimal place and guards against division by zero
Player is prompted to save the report after the on-screen trip report displays

content.body: The final piece: one new function in function_library.py. display_numbered_list() takes any list and a title string, prints the title with a separator, and displays each item with a number prefix. This is the last Module 3 library addition — after this week, the library has eight functions across four categories.

Right now, any numbered list in the game requires the same for-loop boilerplate written out every time. Wrapping it in a library function means changing the display format once changes it everywhere — the route display, the hazard list, any future list you add. That is what library functions are for. Eight functions, three modules. You have built something real.

content.quote: Eight functions, three weeks. That is a library. Every one of those came from a real pain point in the game — a place where you said 'this is annoying to repeat.' Keep that instinct. It is how good code gets written.

items: Open function_library.py. Add a new section comment at the bottom: # LIST UTILITY FUNCTIONS (Added in Lab 3-3)
Define display_numbered_list(items, title). Give it a complete docstring that describes both parameters.
Inside the function: print the title string, then print a separator using '-' * len(title).
Use for i in range(len(items)): to loop through the list by index. Print each item as f' {i + 1}. {items[i]}'.
Print a blank line after the last item so the output has breathing room.
Back in lab-3-3.py, update the final trip report to use function_library.display_numbered_list() for displaying the visited cities route and the sorted hazards list.

rows: The separator line uses '-' * len(title) — it automatically matches the title width, so any title you pass in gets a perfectly fitted separator.
Use range(len(items)) for the loop, not for item in items. You need the index number i to print i + 1 in front of each item.
Test the function by passing it a short list directly in the terminal before wiring it into the game — faster feedback loop.
Don't forget the trailing blank line (print()) at the end of the function. CodeGrade tests the output format exactly, including spacing.

references.description: Shows a function that accepts a list as a parameter and processes it — the same pattern as display_numbered_list().

items: Play a complete game from start to beach. Travel through at least two cities and trigger at least two hazards.
Verify the on-screen trip report shows a numbered city route and a sorted, numbered hazard list.
Choose to save the trip report. Open trip_report.txt in a text editor and confirm it matches the expected format.
Run the program again, load a saved game, play one more turn, and verify the full save-reload-play-report cycle works end to end.
Run python3 function_library.py in your terminal — it should produce no errors.

content.output:
ROUTE:
--------------------
  1. San Antonio
  2. Houston
  3. Galveston

HAZARDS ENCOUNTERED (A-Z):
---------------------------
  1. Armadillo crossing!
  2. Buc-ee's appears!

items: display_numbered_list(items, title) defined in function_library.py with a docstring
Function prints title, separator, numbered items using range(len(items)), and a trailing blank line
Trip report on screen uses function_library.display_numbered_list() for the route and hazard sections
trip_report.txt is generated with the correct multi-section format
Both lab-3-3.py and function_library.py are ready to submit

beforeYouSubmit: lab-3-3.py has a top-of-file comment block with your name, abc123, section number, and a description
function_library.py has display_numbered_list(items, title) with a complete docstring
HAZARD_NAMES list constant defined with at least six hazard strings
REPORT_FILE = 'trip_report.txt' constant defined and used in save_trip_report()
get_random_hazard() uses random index into HAZARD_NAMES — no if/elif chain
Sorted hazard display uses .copy() then .sort() — original hazards_list is not modified
Unique cities are calculated with a for loop and in operator — no sort() or set() on visited_cities
save_trip_report() writes all four sections: header, ROUTE, STATISTICS, HAZARDS (A-Z)
Escape rate calculation guards against division by zero
Player is offered the option to save the trip report at the end of the game
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 7 list methods are all in scope: append(), sort(), copy(), remove(), index(), and len(). The with statement remains permitted for file operations. Do NOT use sets, dictionaries, classes, list comprehensions, or any Chapter 8+ features. The set() type is the Module 4 upgrade for unique tracking — use the for/in approach this week.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–7 (all techniques from Chapters 2–6 plus full Chapter 7 list processing)
specific: list.copy() to duplicate a list before sorting
list.sort() to sort a list in place
for loop with range(len(list)) for indexed display
in operator to check membership and build unique lists
random.randint(0, len(list) - 1) for random index generation
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: set(), dict, classes, list comprehensions, sorted() built-in (use .sort() on a copy instead)

warnings: Submit both lab-3-3.py and function_library.py. A missing function_library.py will fail the display_numbered_list() tests even if your main file is correct.
Do not call sort() directly on hazards_list or visited_cities in the game loop — only sort copies, and only in the report section.

ifYouGetStuck: If trip_report.txt looks wrong, add print statements inside save_trip_report() to trace what is being written before you open the file
Re-read Program 7-12 for the pattern of passing a list to a function and processing it — display_numbered_list() follows the same shape
Submit to CodeGrade early — it tests display_numbered_list() in isolation, so you will see immediately if the function signature or output format is off
Come to Zoom or office hours — MASTER week bugs are almost always a matter of tracking which variable holds the right data at the right moment
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 6 and Chapter 7 BookEx Reference Guides — both chapters remain in scope this week
